labels.intro: Track the watchlist, search the timeline, and keep your current position stored locally in this browser.

items.title: Doctor Strange
items.what: Neurosurgeon Stephen Strange loses the use of his hands in a car accident and finds his way to the mystic arts.
items.why: The introduction of magic as a serious force in the MCU. Other dimensions, the Sanctum Sanctorum, the Eye of Agamotto — without this film, MoM is incomprehensible. Strange is one of the central figures in Doomsday.
items.when: Before Infinity War. The magic logic needs to be in place.
items.who: Benedict Cumberbatch as Stephen Strange, Tilda Swinton as the Ancient One.

items.title: Spider-Man: Homecoming
items.what: Peter Parker tries to earn his place as a superhero in the shadow of Tony Stark, while keeping his high school life together.
items.why: The Stark/Parker dynamic is emotionally the core of Doomsday's "face" storyline. When Doctor Doom appears wearing Stark's face, it's Peter's reaction that matters. Homecoming provides that context.
items.when: Before Infinity War and Far From Home.
items.who: Tom Holland as Peter Parker, Robert Downey Jr. as Tony Stark.

items.title: Black Panther
items.what: T'Challa returns to Wakanda after his father's death and must defend his throne while a dangerous rival wants to open the nation up to war.
items.why: Wakanda is a global player in Doomsday. Vibranium, the technology, the political autonomy. Shuri and Namor — both confirmed for Doomsday — become easier to understand in later films with this one as their foundation.
items.who: Chadwick Boseman as T'Challa, Letitia Wright as Shuri, Michael B. Jordan as Killmonger.

items.title: Black Widow
items.what: Natasha Romanoff confronts her past as she dismantles a dangerous programme that controls dozens of trained widows.
items.why: The introduction of Yelena Belova. She is one of the central figures in Thunderbolts and returns in Doomsday. Red Guardian also appears here for the first time. The family dynamic between Natasha and Yelena gives the Thunderbolts storyline its emotional depth.
items.when: Set before Infinity War, but watch it after Endgame in this order.
items.who: Scarlett Johansson as Natasha, Florence Pugh as Yelena, David Harbour as Red Guardian.

items.title: Ant-Man and the Wasp ★
items.what: Scott Lang and Hope van Dyne enter the Quantum Realm to rescue Hope's mother, while an unstable woman tries to stop them.
items.why: The Quantum Realm is the key to Endgame's time travel solution and the foundation of the multiverse's mechanics. Ghost plays a role later in Thunderbolts. The post-credits scene connects directly to Infinity War.
items.who: Paul Rudd as Scott Lang, Evangeline Lilly as Hope van Dyne, Hannah John-Kamen as Ghost.

items.title: Thor: Ragnarok ★
items.what: Thor discovers Odin has vanished, loses Mjolnir to Hela and must fight his way back through Sakaar to save Asgard.
items.why: The direct bridge to Infinity War. Ragnarok explains why Thor begins his fight against Thanos without his hammer, with one eye and with the survivors of Asgard aboard a refugee ship. Loki's choice involving the Tesseract makes the opening scene of Infinity War logical and emotionally heavier. It also shows that even the realms of gods can fall — exactly the kind of power vacuum that Doom will later exploit.
items.when: Directly before Infinity War. The final scene runs straight into the opening scene of Infinity War.
items.who: Chris Hemsworth as Thor, Tom Hiddleston as Loki, Cate Blanchett as Hela, Tessa Thompson as Valkyrie, Mark Ruffalo as Hulk.

items.title: Avengers: Infinity War ★
items.what: Thanos collects the Infinity Stones to destroy half of all life in the universe. The Avengers fail.
items.why: The first true multiverse-scale threat. The destruction Thanos unleashes is the benchmark against which all later threats — including Doom — are measured. You need to feel what it is like to lose.
items.when: After all of the above films.
items.who: The full Avengers ensemble, Josh Brolin as Thanos.

items.title: Avengers: Endgame ★
items.what: Five years after the Snap, the surviving Avengers attempt to retrieve the Infinity Stones through time travel. Tony Stark sacrifices himself.
items.why: Everything. Stark's sacrifice is the emotional anchor of the entire story through to Doomsday. The time travel creates the multiverse fractures that drive the entire Multiverse Saga. Sam Wilson becomes Captain America here. Endgame is the end of one era and the beginning of everything that follows.
items.who: The full ensemble. Robert Downey Jr. as Tony Stark, Chris Evans as Steve Rogers, Anthony Mackie as Sam Wilson.

items.title: Spider-Man: Far From Home ★
items.what: Peter Parker tries to go on holiday after Endgame, but is pulled back into action. A new threat appears — or seems to.
items.why: The direct emotional aftermath of Endgame. Peter is grieving Stark. The world is grieving Stark. The film poses the question that Doomsday will answer: who takes on that role now? The post-credits scene has direct consequences for No Way Home.
items.who: Tom Holland as Peter Parker, Jake Gyllenhaal as Mysterio.

items.title: WandaVision ★
items.what: Wanda Maximoff, consumed by grief, creates a fictional reality in the town of Westview, modelled on old sitcoms. The outside world tries to break through.
items.why: The birth of the Scarlet Witch. Chaos Magic is fully displayed here for the first time. Without WandaVision, Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness makes no sense — and without MoM, you won't understand Incursions. This is the emotional and technical foundation of the entire magic storyline.
items.when: After Far From Home, before Falcon & The Winter Soldier.
items.who: Elizabeth Olsen as Wanda/Scarlet Witch, Paul Bettany as Vision.

items.title: Falcon & The Winter Soldier ★
items.what: Sam Wilson and Bucky Barnes work together — not always smoothly — while Sam wrestles with whether he can and should carry Steve Rogers' shield.
items.why: Sam Wilson officially becomes Captain America here — which is his position in Doomsday. Bucky's arc continues in Thunderbolts. Valentina Allegra de Fontaine appears here and begins her recruitment game.
items.who: Anthony Mackie as Sam Wilson, Sebastian Stan as Bucky, Julia Louis-Dreyfus as Valentina.

items.title: Hawkeye
items.what: Clint Barton tries to have a quiet Christmas in New York but is confronted with his past as Ronin. Kate Bishop forces her way into his life.
items.why: Introduction of Kate Bishop as the next Hawkeye and one of the faces of the Young Avengers. Yelena Belova returns — her confrontation with Clint is emotionally charged and relevant to her character development heading into Thunderbolts.
items.who: Jeremy Renner as Clint Barton, Hailee Steinfeld as Kate Bishop, Florence Pugh as Yelena.

items.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
items.what: Shang-Chi is confronted by his father, the immortal Xu Wenwu, and a world behind a gate that turns out to be far more dangerous than expected.
items.why: The Ten Rings emit signals into other dimensions. The gateway to Ta Lo opens a dimensional layer relevant to the broader multiverse storylines. Shang-Chi is confirmed for Doomsday.
items.who: Simu Liu as Shang-Chi, Tony Leung as Xu Wenwu.

items.title: Thor: Love and Thunder
items.what: Thor searches for his place in the universe while a former love interest reclaims his hammer and a God Butcher threatens the pantheons.
items.why: Thor becomes a father figure to Gorr's daughter here. That emotional stake — something to protect — gives his presence in Doomsday weight. Contextually the lightest film in the list, but the character development counts.
items.who: Chris Hemsworth as Thor, Natalie Portman as Jane Foster/Mighty Thor, Christian Bale as Gorr.

items.title: Loki — Season 1 & 2 ★
items.what: A variant version of Loki is captured by the Time Variance Authority. He discovers the TVA is hiding far more than it lets on.
items.why: The single most essential series for Doomsday. The TVA, the concept of variants, the Sacred Timeline, and what happens when timelines fall out of balance — all of it is explained here. Season 2 brings Loki to a sacrifice that fundamentally changes the structure of the multiverse. Loki is confirmed for Doomsday.
items.when: Start of Block 3, before everything that follows.
items.who: Tom Hiddleston as Loki, Owen Wilson as Mobius, Sophia Di Martino as Sylvie.

items.title: Moon Knight
items.what: Marc Spector discovers he is the avatar of the Egyptian moon god Khonshu, while also living with dissociative identity disorder.
items.why: Dark magic and divine politics that sit outside the Avengers framework. References to Latveria are subtle but present. Contextually relevant to Doom's world of mystical forces operating beyond the visible MCU.
items.when: After Loki, before No Way Home.
items.who: Oscar Isaac as Marc Spector/Steven Grant/Moon Knight.

items.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home ★
items.what: Peter asks Doctor Strange to cast a spell that makes the world forget who Spider-Man is. The spell goes wrong and opens the door to other universes.
items.why: Reality fractures here for the first time as a direct result of human action — no cosmic entity, just a teenager botching a spell. This is the moment the multiverse shifts from theory to threat.
items.who: Tom Holland as Peter Parker, Benedict Cumberbatch as Strange, three generations of Spider-Man.

items.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness ★
items.what: Strange travels through the multiverse to protect America Chavez, while the Scarlet Witch attempts to destroy every version of herself that stands in her way.
items.why: The word Incursion is introduced here in earnest for the first time. An Incursion is what happens when two universes physically collide and one or both are destroyed. This is the core of what Doom threatens to cause or exploit in Doomsday. Without understanding this, you will miss the stakes of the film entirely.
items.who: Benedict Cumberbatch as Strange, Elizabeth Olsen as Wanda, Xochitl Gomez as America Chavez.

items.title: Ant-Man and the Wasp: Quantumania
items.what: Scott Lang and his family are pulled into the Quantum Realm and come face to face with Kang the Conqueror, a time-travelling ruler who wants to control the universe.
items.why: The Kang storyline has officially been replaced by Doctor Doom as Doomsday's primary antagonist. Even so, Quantumania provides context for the scale of a multiversal power struggle. The concepts overlap with what Doom is building toward.
items.who: Paul Rudd as Scott Lang, Jonathan Majors as Kang.

items.title: Black Panther: Wakanda Forever ★
items.what: Wakanda mourns T'Challa while a new threat emerges from the underwater world of Talokan, led by the immortal Namor.
items.why: Namor is confirmed for Doomsday. His introduction here — his motivations, his protection of Talokan, his willingness to use violence — is essential. Ironheart also makes her debut. Wakanda's politics and its alliance with Talokan factor into the global balance of power heading into Doomsday.
items.who: Letitia Wright as Shuri, Tenoch Huerta as Namor, Dominique Thorne as Ironheart.

items.title: The Marvels
items.what: Carol Danvers, Kamala Khan and Monica Rambeau are linked through their powers and forced to work together while involuntarily swapping places.
items.why: Universes begin leaking here. The post-credits scene introduces a direct X-Men connection that explains Doomsday's broader cast. The lightest film in this block, but the multiverse consequences are real.
items.who: Brie Larson as Carol Danvers, Teyonah Parris as Monica, Iman Vellani as Kamala Khan.

items.title: Deadpool & Wolverine ★
items.what: Wade Wilson is recruited by the TVA and travels through the Void — a graveyard of dead timelines — alongside a variant version of Logan.
items.why: The most direct Doomsday preparation in terms of terminology. The concept of Anchor Beings — figures whose existence keeps a timeline alive — is fully worked out here. It also officially connects the Fox universe to the MCU, making the return of the original X-Men cast in Doomsday possible.
items.who: Ryan Reynolds as Deadpool, Hugh Jackman as Wolverine.

items.title: Captain America: Brave New World ★
items.what: Sam Wilson operates as the new Captain America while a political conspiracy involving the new US President unfolds.
items.why: Sam Wilson plays a central leadership role in Doomsday. Brave New World establishes his mandate as the new Captain America and shows how he navigates power and political pressure. Bucky's decision to run for Congress carries directly into Thunderbolts. Red Hulk is introduced.
items.who: Anthony Mackie as Sam Wilson, Harrison Ford as President Ross/Red Hulk, Sebastian Stan as Bucky.

items.title: Thunderbolts* ★
items.what: A group of government operatives — Yelena, Bucky, Ghost, Red Guardian, Taskmaster, John Walker — are assembled for a mission that spirals into something far larger.
items.why: This team is Earth's new line of defence. They are not the Avengers — they are morally grey, broken and working for people with hidden agendas. The film is a direct Doomsday setup: what this team does and who they become determines, in part, how the world withstands Doom's attack.
items.who: Florence Pugh as Yelena, Sebastian Stan as Bucky, David Harbour as Red Guardian, Julia Louis-Dreyfus as Valentina.

items.title: The Fantastic Four: First Steps ★
items.what: The Fantastic Four operate in a retro-futuristic 1960s world on an alternate Earth and face a cosmic threat.
items.why: Arguably the most direct Doomsday setup of any film in this block. The Fantastic Four's greatest enemy in the comics is Doctor Doom. Victor von Doom and Reed Richards know each other. That relationship is the backbone of what happens in Doomsday.
items.who: Pedro Pascal as Reed Richards, Vanessa Kirby as Sue Storm, Joseph Quinn as Johnny Storm, Ebon Moss-Bachrach as Ben Grimm.

items.title: Wonder Man
items.what: Simon Williams, actor and former Hydra operative, discovers that his ionic energy grants him extraordinary abilities while Hollywood turns out to be a very strange stage.
items.why: Wonder Man introduces ionic energy as a power type in the MCU — a source that may prove relevant in the broader multiverse conflict. Contextually, it fills out the picture of how new forces and figures are emerging in the lead-up to Doomsday.
items.who: Yahya Abdul-Mateen II as Simon Williams/Wonder Man.

items.title: Daredevil: Born Again — Season 2
items.what: Matt Murdock operates as Daredevil in a New York that is growing increasingly politically unstable, while Kingpin tightens his grip on the city.
items.why: Political chaos as the backdrop for Doomsday. Valentina's network and Kingpin's control of New York form a subplot relevant to the broader power structure. Chronologically the most recent MCU title — set in approximately 2027.
items.when: After Wonder Man, shortly before Spider-Man: Brand New Day.
items.who: Charlie Cox as Matt Murdock/Daredevil, Vincent D'Onofrio as Kingpin.

items.title: VisionQuest
items.what: White Vision attempts to recover his memories and reclaim his humanity. Inside his mind, other AI programs from the MCU appear in human form — including Ultron, locked behind a firewall.
items.why: Closes the trilogy that began with WandaVision and continued with Agatha All Along. Vision is a direct product of Stark technology and the Mind Stone — thematically intertwined with the AI and identity questions that Doomsday confronts. Multiple watchlist sources flag it as essential for Doomsday context.
items.when: After Daredevil: Born Again S2, before Spider-Man: Brand New Day. Note: the exact release date within 2026 has not yet been confirmed.
items.who: Paul Bettany as White Vision, James Spader as Ultron.

items.title: Spider-Man: Brand New Day ★
items.what: Peter Parker rebuilds his life. The world has forgotten him following the spell in No Way Home. He has to start over — new friends, new enemies, new identity.
items.why: The last MCU film before Doomsday. Peter's position as someone who has been literally erased from the world's memory places him in a unique role when Doom's attack comes. Thematically, it closes the circle opened in Far From Home.
items.who: Tom Holland as Peter Parker.

items.title: Avengers: Doomsday ★
items.what: The Avengers, the Fantastic Four, returning X-Men figures and other MCU players converge as Victor von Doom enters the story on a multiversal scale.
items.why: This is the destination of the entire watchlist. Every thread leads here: Stark's legacy, Doom's connection to the Fantastic Four, the instability of the multiverse, the TVA context, and the teams formed across Phases 4 through 6.
items.when: Watch after everything else in this guide. This is the endpoint, not preparation.
items.who: Directed by Joe and Anthony Russo. Robert Downey Jr. is listed in the cast, alongside multiple returning MCU and legacy Marvel actors on Marvel's official page.

items.title: X-Men (2000)
items.what: Mutants are threatened in a world that refuses to accept them. Professor Xavier and Magneto choose opposing paths.
items.why: The original introduction of the ensemble: Xavier, Magneto, Cyclops, Storm, Jean Grey, Wolverine. The dynamic between Charles and Erik is the emotional core of everything that follows.

items.title: X2: X-Men United (2003)
items.what: A military assault on the X-Mansion forces the X-Men to work alongside Magneto.
items.why: The best film in the Fox trilogy. Character depth, action and moral complexity at their strongest. Provides the most context for the returning characters in Doomsday.

items.title: X-Men: Days of Future Past (2014)
items.what: Wolverine travels back in time to prevent an attack that will destroy the future.
items.why: Thematically the most relevant Fox film for Doomsday. Timeline manipulation, the erasure of a future, the idea that the past determines what exists now — precisely the concepts that will be central to Doomsday.
